rationales.achievement: He stresses that he used to manage "forty people" and still knows "how to negotiate, how to plan logistics," framing himself as highly capable and accomplished.
rationales.power: The entry centers on lost status and authority, pushing back against being treated like "delivery drivers don't know anything" and asserting that his former standing still matters.

rationales.hedonism: He takes unwanted shifts until "2 AM" and says keeping the smile on for ratings "takes a lot of energy," showing comfort and enjoyment being sacrificed.
rationales.achievement: He tracks earnings, maps which restaurants have the fastest pickup times, and works to keep a "4.92" rating for Diamond status, showing strong performance orientation.
rationales.security: He focuses on "more orders and fewer accidents," savings lasting "three months, maybe four," and priority access meaning "more consistent income," all aimed at stability.
rationales.conformity: Maintaining the required rating depends on customer-facing behavior, and he explicitly notes the strain of having to "keep the smile on" to meet those expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects pressure to "explore other options" and insists, "I'm not going to apologize" and "I'm adapting," asserting his own judgment about how to live and work.
rationales.security: He frames his choices as "doing what needs to be done to keep this family running," emphasizing practical stability under threat.
rationales.conformity: He refuses to fall in line with his brother's and son's implied expectations about respectable work, explicitly saying he will not apologize for his path.
rationales.benevolence: His defense of gig work is rooted in supporting close family, saying he is doing what is necessary "to keep this family running."

rationales.power: He fixates on markers of rank and deference like "my old office," "the chair," and people knocking before entering, showing the importance of status and respect.

rationales.achievement: He is intensely focused on measured performance, describing how "three weeks of perfect service" disappeared when his rating fell to "4.88" because of someone else's mistake.

rationales.hedonism: He works from "11 AM" to midnight, notes "My back hurts from the scooter," and puts off replacing the seat, showing comfort and relief being deferred.
rationales.security: He avoids spending "NT$1,500" on the seat because he does not want to spend it "right now," indicating a protective, scarcity-aware approach to money.

rationales.hedonism: He works on Christmas for surge pricing, sits outside a restaurant "instead of going inside where it's warm," and gives up comfort and celebration.
rationales.power: He keeps returning to hierarchy and diminished standing, contrasting his former place in a "clear chain of command" with being reduced by the app to "three numbers."
rationales.security: He chooses holiday work because "the surge pricing is excellent" and says the job feels like "standing on ice that might crack any second," showing strong concern with economic precariousness.
rationales.benevolence: He prioritizes work over being home with his wife on Christmas, even after she sends a photo of the dinner and the "table looked empty with only one place setting out."

rationales.hedonism: He comes home to cold food after working late, and the entry centers on deprivation rather than enjoyment: "The food was cold but I ate it anyway."

rationales.achievement: He remembers being promoted to "assistant manager" and having a clear climb to "Director by 45, maybe VP by 50," showing strong investment in success and recognized competence.
rationales.power: He reacts sharply to being called "Uncle" by a wealthier younger customer and contrasts it with his old upward trajectory, revealing sensitivity to rank and status loss.
rationales.security: He says "Everyone should" worry about money and frames his son's job and his own work through financial vulnerability and planning.
rationales.benevolence: Even while struggling, he tells his son "that's great, focus on your studies, don't worry about money," prioritizing his son's wellbeing and prospects.

rationales.achievement: He sets and reaches a concrete performance target, noting he finally hit "Diamond Driver status" by maintaining a "4.91 rating" for two months.
rationales.security: The value of Diamond status is that "Priority orders start Monday" and it means "more consistent orders," which he treats as economic stability.

rationales.hedonism: He creates a pleasurable dinner with wine, then undermines it by calculating that the time "could have been two hours in the library," so enjoyment is treated as something needing justification.
rationales.achievement: His mind immediately returns to the "lit review chapter that's already a week behind," showing performance demands overriding the meal.

rationales.hedonism: He uses a free hour to sit in the Grote Markt with "a stroopwafel" and watch people, and says the small break left him feeling better than he had all week.
Even though he knew a nap would help, he denied himself rest and ended the day depleted, saying he "ate cereal standing up."
rationales.achievement: He pushes himself to keep working on the methods chapter despite exhaustion, judging the day through dissertation progress and peer comparison.
rationales.conformity: He stays because of how it would look for "the oldest person in the programme" to go home and nap, showing behavior shaped by perceived social expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to turn the moment into another self-monitoring exercise, saying, "I'm not going to overanalyse listening to a record at midnight."
rationales.hedonism: He chooses simple sensory pleasure by putting on a Bill Evans record and sitting with it, treating the music itself as sufficient.
He enjoys the appelgebak slowly and notes there was "No guilt arithmetic this time," allowing himself uncomplicated pleasure.
rationales.achievement: The day centers on productive archive work, and in the response he explicitly says the cake felt acceptable because it came "after the work" and felt "earned."

rationales.hedonism: He wants to enjoy "just a beer on his terrace" but cannot stop the internal tally of what leisure is "costing," which blocks enjoyment in the moment.
rationales.achievement: His inability to relax is driven by concern that the "chapter draft is still rough" before Monday's supervisor meeting, showing work performance dominating the evening.

rationales.hedonism: He takes quiet pleasure in ordinary things like making pasta, watching a documentary, and concluding, "Sometimes that's fine."
rationales.achievement: He describes the day as "Productive enough" and notes he drafted "two pages that might actually survive the next revision," showing steady investment in competent work.

rationales.hedonism: He knowingly gives up a meal with a good friend that he admits he "would have enjoyed," then ends with "a sandwich at my desk" and a sense of bleakness.
rationales.achievement: He chooses revision and reading over lunch because of the ever-present "deadline," even while acknowledging the work was not truly urgent.

rationales.self_direction: He deliberately chooses not to go "straight to the laptop" and tells himself, "The thesis will be there in the morning," asserting control over his own evening.
rationales.hedonism: The entry is centered on comfort and sensory enjoyment: a "proper bath," bath oil, tea, and the recognition that "Tonight, I think, this was enough."
